china_pn3_note: Press Note 3 (April 2020) requires prior Government of India approval for any FDI from land-border countries, and approvals for Chinese proposals are rare and slow. This reshapes what 'India investment interest' can look like for Chinese corporates: (1) outright greenfield/JV proposals get rejected — BYD-Megha's $1bn EV plant (2023) is the canonical case; (2) incumbents 'Indianize' — SAIC diluted MG Motor India to JSW (49%→39% and falling), Haier sold 49% of Haier India to Bharti/Warburg for ~$2bn (Dec 2025, cleared by Beijing) explicitly to escape PN3 friction; (3) Indian-majority JV fronts carry the manufacturing — Prama Hikvision; (4) technology licensing/royalty (CATL's LRS model) is the theoretically PN3-compatible route but is now ALSO constrained from the Chinese side by Beijing's export controls on battery/rare-earth/process technology (the Reliance-CATL talks died partly on this); (5) pre-2020 grandfathered assets (Midea's Pune park, Luxshare's Tamil Nadu plant, BYD Chennai) become the only expansion platforms; (6) asset-light models — export/dealer networks (Gree, Baosteel, Wanhua sales office) and services (COSCO liner calls) — proceed freely because they involve no Indian FDI. Net effect: annual reports of Chinese majors show India as a fast-growing REVENUE geography (Haier +30%, 'developing countries' +30% for Mindray) while capex conspicuously routes to Vietnam, Thailand, Indonesia, Brazil, Hungary, Saudi Arabia instead. ‖ UPDATE (Press Note 2 of 2026, Cabinet 15-Mar-2026, verified via RS UQ3877 27-Mar-2026 + India Briefing/Vistra/legal advisories): PN3 is now PARTIALLY relaxed. Foreign companies in which land-border investors hold <=10% on a NON-CONTROLLING basis (no board seat, no veto, no operational control) may invest via the AUTOMATIC route in otherwise-permitted sectors, subject to a beneficial-ownership test + post-investment intimation (~60-day processing). TARGETING IMPLICATION: the cohort below is no longer flatly blocked. (a) A NEW lane opens for PASSIVE MINORITY Chinese capital -- financial-investor stakes, <=10% strategic holdings, and funds with sub-10% Chinese LP exposure that PN3 had swept in; (b) CONTROLLING stakes, greenfield plants and JVs with board control STILL need government approval, so BYD-Megha-type proposals remain gated; (c) the beneficial-ownership test means a European/Japanese target with a small Chinese shareholder is now cleaner to court than before. So the actionable read is: Chinese majors still can't build controlled plants, but minority co-investment and supply-chain equity are now automatic-route -- a real, narrow new targeting lane. Gazette text not fetched directly; read it before advising on a specific deal.
